name: Deceased pharaohs and souls
shortDescription: Represents the collective souls of deceased pharaohs and other worthy individuals who have passed into the Duat, often associated with navigating the afterlife and achieving eternal life.
description: In Egyptian mythology, the deceased pharaohs and other worthy souls held a significant position, particularly in relation to the Duat, the Egyptian underworld. Upon death, the pharaoh, considered a divine ruler, underwent mummification rituals to preserve the body for the afterlife. The soul, comprised of elements like the Ba (personality) and Ka (life force), embarked on a perilous journey through the Duat, facing trials and demons. The ultimate goal was to pass the Weighing of the Heart ceremony, overseen by Anubis and Thoth, where the heart was weighed against the feather of Ma'at (truth and justice). A successful passage ensured entry into the Field of Reeds (Aaru), a paradise mirroring earthly life. The deceased pharaohs were believed to join Ra on his solar barque, assisting in the daily battle against Apophis, the serpent of chaos, ensuring the sun's rise and the continuation of cosmic order. They were not simply passive recipients of eternal life, but active participants in maintaining the balance of the universe. The collective souls of the worthy deceased also served as intermediaries between the living and the gods, capable of influencing events in the mortal realm. The belief in the afterlife and the importance of preparing for it heavily influenced Egyptian culture, architecture (pyramids and tombs), and funerary practices. The preservation of the pharaoh's name and memory was crucial for their continued existence in the afterlife, hence the elaborate inscriptions and depictions within their tombs. The state of the deceased pharaohs and souls was intrinsically tied to the health and prosperity of Egypt. Their continued existence ensured the cyclical renewal of life and the triumph over chaos.

sources.title: Egyptian Ideas of the Future Life
sources.author: E. A. Wallis Budge
